attributes.stem:
A 59-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic with a three-day history of painful swelling and redness of her right index finger. She reports a burning sensation and notices small fluid-filled blisters. She has type 2 diabetes mellitus managed with metformin and gliclazide. She also takes ramipril for hypertension. She has had a similar episode previously but did not seek medical attention and it resolved after three weeks.

On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. Her right index finger is erythematous and swollen with grouped vesicles on an erythematous base. The affected area is tender to touch. There is no lymphadenopathy or fever.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate management?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with clinical features consistent with herpetic whitlow, a herpes simplex virus infection of the finger from HSV-1 or HSV-2. The presentation of grouped vesicles, burning pain, and previous similar episode are characteristic. Patients may have a history of previous herpes infection (spread via contact with oral or genital herpes), an occupation that exposes them to oral secretions, or children who suck their thumbs when they have a herpes infection.

Diagnosis is usually clinical and patients are advised to cover the lesions to prevent spread of the virus. Given her diabetes, which affects immune function, systemic antiviral therapy is indicated and to shorten symptom duration.

Oral acyclovir is the most appropriate treatment as it provides systemic coverage needed for immunocompromised patients. Early treatment can prevent complications such as secondary bacterial infection and disseminated infection. The typical regime is 400mg three times daily for 7-10 days.

Incision and drainage is contraindicated in herpetic whitlow as it may delay healing, increase pain, and risk secondary bacterial infection. This procedure might be appropriate for a bacterial felon but would worsen outcomes in viral infection.

Topical acyclovir alone would be insufficient for this patient given her diabetes. While topical antivirals could be considered for immunocompetent patients with mild disease, systemic therapy is required here.

Oral flucloxacillin would be appropriate for bacterial paronychia but not for a primary viral infection. There are currently no signs of bacterial superinfection that would warrant antibacterial therapy.

Warm compresses alone are inadequate treatment. While they might provide symptomatic relief, they do not address the underlying viral infection, particularly in an immunocompromised patient who requires systemic therapy.
learningPoints: Herpetic whitlow requires systemic antiviral therapy in immunocompromised patients including those with diabetes.
Incision and drainage is contraindicated in herpetic whitlow as it may delay healing and increase risk of secondary bacterial infection.
references: Wong V, Couslson I. Herpetic whitlow. DermNet NZ. 2023. Available at: https://dermnetnz.org/topics/herpetic-whitlow
Ismail A, Gold W. Herpetic Whitlow in a 39-year-old woman. CAnadian Medical Association Journal. 2020: 192 (35). Available at: https://www.cmaj.ca/content/192/35/e1010
